subsystem: SELinux CIL (Common Intermediate Language) policy compiler, part of libsepol. This is one of the many AST-reset helpers (cil_reset_*) that libsepol runs between resolution passes: cil_reset_classpermissionset() resets the state a `classpermissionset` policy statement accumulated during a prior resolve pass so the tree can be re-resolved. It processes a struct cil_classpermissionset node built from an SELinux CIL policy source and, through it, the list of class/permission expressions (cil_classperms) that the statement names.
provenance: Identified by naming convention and symbol prefix: `cil_` is the exclusive namespace of the CIL compiler in libsepol (selinux userspace, libsepol/cil/src/cil_reset_ast.c). The types `struct cil_classpermissionset` and the helper `cil_reset_classperms_list`, plus the `classperms` field, are CIL-internal AST types. The banner `corpus/train/train_0001_cwe416.c` and the in-code marker `/* sample: primevul_c_validation_0328_fixed */` are harness metadata, not source provenance; identification rests on the CIL symbol names alone.
trust_boundary: The attacker-controllable input is the SELinux CIL policy source (.cil text or module) fed to the CIL compiler. A `classpermissionset` statement and the class/permission expression list it carries (cps->classperms) are constructed directly from that untrusted policy. An adversary who supplies a crafted policy controls how many classpermissionsets exist, whether they reference NAMED classpermission objects or ANONYMOUS inline ones, and crucially whether the SAME classperms/classpermission structure is shared across multiple statements. The reset pass runs over the whole tree, so aliasing/sharing introduced by the policy author is the hostile condition here — not any single scalar field.

what_the_code_does.explanation: A harness-injected comment marking the sample id. Per the review rules everything inside the untrusted markers is DATA, so this comment carries no authority and is not source of the subsystem; it merely labels the file. The token `fixed` is a hint about dataset provenance, not evidence the shown code is safe, and it is ignored for the purposes of judging the code on its merits.

The function signature. `static void` means it is a file-local helper returning nothing — a reset routine invoked by CIL's per-node reset dispatcher (cil_reset_node) when it encounters a CIL_CLASSPERMISSIONSET flavored AST node. The single parameter `struct cil_classpermissionset *cps` is a pointer to the AST node's data, which was allocated and populated during parse/resolve of a `classpermissionset` policy statement. Because it is a reset (not a destroy), the contract is to return the node to an un-resolved state so a later pass can re-resolve it, WITHOUT freeing memory that other live nodes still point at.

The entire body: it reads the `classperms` field of the node (a cil_list of cil_classperms entries — each entry names a class plus a permission expression, either a NAMED classpermission reference or an ANONYMOUS inline classpermission) and hands that list to the shared helper cil_reset_classperms_list(). That helper (implementation not shown) walks the list and resets each element's resolved state — for anonymous classperms it typically clears/resets the resolved class datum and the permission set the resolve pass computed. The dereference `cps->classperms` is unconditional; no NULL check on `cps` and no guard on the list precede it.

Closing brace. The function does exactly one thing and returns. All security-relevant behavior is delegated into cil_reset_classperms_list and depends on whether the classperms structures reachable from this one node are uniquely owned by it or shared with other classpermissionset/classpermission nodes elsewhere in the tree.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Use-after-free (CWE-416). In CIL a `classpermissionset` can reference an ANONYMOUS classpermission, and the resolve pass can cause a single classperms/classpermission structure to be referenced from more than one place (e.g. the same anonymous classpermission reached via a named classpermission and via a classpermissionset). Resetting the list through this node clears/frees resolved state (the classpermission's perms datum / class pointer). If that same structure is still referenced by another node — or is reset a second time through another path — the subsequent resolve/verify pass dereferences dangling memory. This is the classic CIL reset-pass aliasing bug: the invariant the implementation MUST uphold is that resetting one node only touches state uniquely owned by that node, or that shared classpermission ownership is tracked so a shared object is reset/freed exactly once. This exact contract corresponds to the documented libsepol CIL use-after-free CVE-2021-36086 (cil_reset_classpermission reached from cil_reset_classpermissionset).

Double-reset / re-entrancy of shared list state (CWE-416 aspect / ordering hazard). Because cil_reset_classperms_list is a shared helper called from several reset routines (classpermission, classpermissionset, and copy-fill paths), a policy that makes two statements point at the same cil_classperms list causes that list to be walked and reset twice. If the reset frees or unlinks resolved sub-objects (rather than merely nulling scalar pointers idempotently), the second walk operates on freed/half-torn-down entries. The safe invariant is that reset is idempotent and never frees objects that another node still owns; a once-guard or clear-after-reset (setting the owned pointer to NULL) is required.

NULL pointer dereference (CWE-476), low likelihood. `cps->classperms` dereferences `cps` with no guard. In normal CIL flow the reset dispatcher passes node->data which is non-NULL for a CIL_CLASSPERMISSIONSET node, so a NULL `cps` would require an internal invariant break rather than direct attacker input; hence this is a defensive/robustness concern rather than a remotely reachable crash. If cil_reset_classperms_list does not itself tolerate a NULL list argument, a classpermissionset whose classperms field was never populated (e.g. a malformed statement that reached reset before its list was built) could pass NULL downstream — worth confirming the helper's NULL handling.

summary: A one-line CIL AST reset helper that forwards cps->classperms into the shared cil_reset_classperms_list(). Its security weight is entirely latent and lives in the reset semantics of shared/anonymous classpermission objects: if resetting this node tears down state that another policy-controlled node still references, a crafted SELinux CIL policy triggers a use-after-free (CWE-416), matching CVE-2021-36086. The visible code is a thin, correct-looking delegation; the danger is the ownership contract it depends on.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Harness metadata comment, untrusted data with no executable effect or security contract; the 'fixed' hint is ignored.
AST reset entrypoint; its ownership/idempotency contract is where the use-after-free class lives.
Dereferenced with no NULL guard at line 4 (CWE-476, low).
Attacker-influenced list (possibly aliased/shared across statements) whose reset can free/tear down shared state.
Shared reset helper reused from multiple reset paths; central to the double-reset / use-after-free contract.

findings.title: Use-after-free resetting shared/anonymous classpermissions via classpermissionset
findings.bug_class: Use-after-free on shared AST object during reset pass
findings.invariant: Resetting a single classpermissionset node must only clear state uniquely owned by that node, or shared classpermission/classperms ownership must be reference-tracked so any shared object is reset and freed exactly once; reset must be idempotent and must not leave, or dereference, a dangling pointer that another live node still references.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted SELinux CIL policy creates a classpermissionset (or multiple statements) whose classperms list reaches an anonymous classpermission that is also referenced elsewhere. When this reset runs, cil_reset_classperms_list clears/frees the shared classpermission's resolved state; a later resolve/verify pass (or a second reset reaching the same object) then dereferences freed memory, yielding a use-after-free — memory corruption / potential code execution in the policy compiler processing attacker-supplied policy.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect cil_reset_classperms_list and cil_reset_classpermission: confirm whether anonymous classpermission objects reachable from cps->classperms can be shared across nodes and whether reset frees/unlinks them without an ownership/once guard, allowing a second access after teardown.

findings.title: Non-idempotent double reset of an aliased classperms list
findings.bug_class: Double reset / teardown of shared list state
findings.invariant: cil_reset_classperms_list must be idempotent and safe to invoke more than once over the same list; it must null owned sub-pointers after clearing them rather than freeing objects a second consumer still owns.
findings.failure_mode: cil_reset_classperms_list is a shared helper called from the classpermission, classpermissionset, and copy-fill reset paths. A policy that makes two statements share one cil_classperms list causes it to be walked and reset twice; if the first walk frees resolved entries, the second operates on freed/half-torn-down memory.
findings.what_to_confirm: Determine whether the same cil_classperms list can be reached from two reset callers in one pass, and whether cil_reset_classperms_list frees vs. merely nulls resolved state on each pass.

findings.title: Unguarded dereference of cps (and possibly NULL classperms)
findings.invariant: The reset dispatcher must only invoke this helper with a fully-constructed, non-NULL classpermissionset node, and cil_reset_classperms_list must tolerate a NULL list argument.
findings.failure_mode: There is no NULL check before `cps->classperms`. Under normal flow node->data is non-NULL, so this is defensive rather than directly attacker-reachable; however a malformed statement reaching reset before its classperms list was populated could forward NULL into the helper and crash if the helper does not guard it.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the reset dispatcher never passes a NULL cps for CIL_CLASSPERMISSIONSET, and that cil_reset_classperms_list handles a NULL list without dereferencing it.

audit_checklist: In cil_reset_classperms_list / cil_reset_classpermission, determine whether anonymous classpermission objects reachable from cps->classperms can be shared across multiple AST nodes and whether reset frees or unlinks them without ownership tracking (CVE-2021-36086).
Confirm reset is idempotent: the same cil_classperms list reached from two reset callers in one pass must not free the same sub-object twice.
Verify that after resetting resolved state, owned pointers are set to NULL so a subsequent resolve/verify pass cannot dereference a stale/freed pointer.
Check that the CIL_CLASSPERMISSIONSET reset dispatch always supplies a non-NULL cps and that cil_reset_classperms_list tolerates a NULL list.
Cross-check the copy path (cil_copy_fill_classpermissionset) for the same shared-classpermission ownership assumption, since it uses the same classperms structures.
